preFix.title: Celeriac and apple soup
preFix.subtitle: Earthy, subtly sweet, and very smooth
preFix.excerpt: Celeriac roasted until golden, blended with a cooking apple and thyme-scented stock. The apple cuts through the earthiness of the celeriac and gives the soup a light, fresh note without making it sweet.
preFix.sourceNotes: Celeriac and apple is a classic French flavour combination, used in salads (celeriac remoulade with apple) and soups. No single canonical source; method synthesised from standard French-British autumn soup approaches.

content.text: Celeriac makes one of the best autumn soups: earthy, smooth, and slightly nutty. The apple ,  sharp enough to cut the celeriac's richness ,  is the pairing that makes it work rather than staying merely pleasant. Roasting the
before blending is optional but recommended: 20 minutes in a hot oven concentrates the flavour and adds a light caramelisation that a boiled base cannot match.

items.name: Celeriac
items.prepNote: peeled thickly and cut into chunks

items.name: Apple
items.prepNote: cooking apple, peeled, cored and diced

content.text: Heat the oven to 200°C conventional. Spread the {{celeriac}} of celeriac chunks on a roasting tin, drizzle with oil, season with salt, and roast for 20 to 25 minutes until golden at the edges.

Melt {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a large saucepan over a medium-low heat. Add the {{onion}} onion and cook for 6 minutes until soft. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic and {{thyme}} of thyme sprigs and cook for 1 minute.
Add the roasted celeriac, the {{apple}} of diced apple, and pour in {{stock-vegetable}} of hot stock. Simmer for 15 minutes until both the celeriac and apple are very tender.
Remove the thyme stalks. Blend until smooth. Taste and season with salt and a little black pepper.
Ladle into bowls. Add a swirl of cream if using.

items.fix: Use a sharp cooking apple such as Bramley. If the soup is already too sweet, a squeeze of lemon juice and a pinch more salt brings it back into balance.
items.cause: Eating apples are much sweeter than cooking apples; or the apple was large.

items.fix: Season the celeriac well before roasting, season the onion as it softens, and taste again after blending. A squeeze of lemon brightens the whole soup.
items.cause: Celeriac is subtle and needs seasoning at every stage.

items.fix: Cut away any very fibrous inner parts before roasting. Blend for longer, and pass through a sieve if you want a completely smooth result.
items.cause: Celeriac can have fibrous strands if the inner core is included.

items.fix: Remove from the oven once golden at the edges. The soup will still be a warm ivory colour; deep browning turns it muddy.
items.cause: The celeriac was roasted for too long and took on heavy browning.

content.text: Celeriac with apple is a classic French pairing, best known in celeriac remoulade where the two are served raw and dressed in mustard mayonnaise. Translated into a soup it becomes one of the more interesting autumn first courses ,  unpredictable enough to prompt questions but familiar enough to comfort. In France, celeriac soup (velouté de céleri-rave) has been a standard of bourgeois home cooking for generations. In Britain it arrived later, via restaurant menus in the 1990s.

initialFindings.message: tricolon: tricolon (three parallel items) — two adjectives almost always beats three
body contains unresolved placeholder {{celeriac}} — must be substituted before publish
